stem: A neonate with DiGeorge syndrome has severe cyanosis. Echocardiogram shows a single arterial trunk overriding both ventricles with a VSD. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Persistent truncus arteriosus: failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to divide the truncus into aorta and pulmonary artery → single great vessel receiving blood from both ventricles (always with a VSD). Neural crest defect — strongly associated with DiGeorge/22q11.2 deletion. Classified by branch pulmonary artery origin (Collett-Edwards or Van Praagh). Treatment: surgical repair in first weeks of life (Rastelli procedure — VSD closure + RV-to-PA conduit).
AP window is a defect between two separately formed great arteries. In truncus, the septation never occurred.
TOF has a separate (though small) pulmonary artery. A single trunk overriding the septum with a VSD suggests a different conotruncal defect.
PDA is a connection between two separate great arteries. Truncus arteriosus means the separation never occurred — only one trunk exists.

explanation: Correct: Truncus arteriosus (persistent).

stem: Which embryologic cell population is responsible for aorticopulmonary septum formation?

choices.feedback: Secondary heart field contributes to the outflow tract myocardium and right ventricle, but the aorticopulmonary septum itself is neural crest-derived.
Endocardial cushions form AV septum and valves. The outflow tract septum requires a different cell population.

Correct! Cardiac neural crest cells migrate from the pharyngeal arch region to form the aorticopulmonary septum — the spiral partition that divides the truncus into aorta and pulmonary artery. Neural crest ablation → conotruncal defects: truncus arteriosus, TOF, D-TGA, interrupted aortic arch, DORV. DiGeorge (22q11.2): neural crest dysfunction → conotruncal defects + thymic aplasia + hypoparathyroidism.
Proepicardial cells contribute to coronary arteries and cardiac fibroblasts, not the aorticopulmonary septum.

stem: A neonate presents with bilious vomiting on day 1 of life. Upper GI series shows a 'corkscrew' pattern of the duodenum. What is the diagnosis and urgent management?

choices.text: Pyloric stenosis — pyloromyotomy after correction of alkalosis
choices.feedback: Pyloric stenosis presents at 2-6 weeks with NON-bilious projectile vomiting. Bilious vomiting on day 1 suggests obstruction BELOW the ampulla of Vater.

choices.text: Duodenal atresia — nasogastric decompression and elective repair
choices.feedback: Duodenal atresia shows a 'double bubble' sign. The corkscrew pattern is specific for malrotation with volvulus — a true emergency.

choices.text: Necrotizing enterocolitis — antibiotics and bowel rest
choices.feedback: NEC typically presents in premature infants after feeding initiation, not day 1 in a term neonate with a corkscrew UGI pattern.

choices.text: Midgut volvulus from malrotation — emergent Ladd procedure
choices.feedback: Correct! Bilious vomiting in a neonate = midgut volvulus until proven otherwise (surgical emergency). Malrotation → narrow SMA mesenteric base → midgut twists → volvulus → intestinal ischemia → can lose entire midgut within hours. Diagnosis: upper GI series (corkscrew/bird's beak duodenum, abnormal DJ junction position). Treatment: emergent Ladd procedure — detorse volvulus, lyse Ladd bands, widen mesenteric base, appendectomy, place cecum in LLQ (intentional non-rotation).

stem: A term neonate fails to pass meconium in the first 48 hours and develops abdominal distension. Rectal exam produces an explosive gush of stool and gas. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Imperforate anus is identified on physical exam — no anal opening visible. This patient had a rectal exam performed, so the anus is patent.
Small left colon (seen in infants of diabetic mothers) can mimic Hirschsprung but usually resolves spontaneously.
Meconium ileus presents with distal ileal obstruction and is associated with CF, but the explosive decompression on rectal exam is more characteristic of Hirschsprung.

Correct! Hirschsprung disease: absence of ganglion cells (Auerbach and Meissner plexuses) in the distal colon → functional obstruction. Classic presentation: failure to pass meconium within 48 hours (99% of healthy term neonates pass meconium by 48 hours) + abdominal distension + explosive decompression on rectal exam. Caused by failure of neural crest cell migration (cranial to caudal — rectosigmoid most common). Transition zone on contrast enema. Diagnosis confirmed: rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells + hypertrophied nerve trunks.

stem: What is the diagnostic gold standard for Hirschsprung disease?

choices.feedback: Manometry shows absent RAIR (rectoanal inhibitory reflex) in Hirschsprung — useful screening tool but biopsy is needed for definitive diagnosis.
Abdominal X-ray may show distal bowel obstruction with dilated loops but cannot distinguish Hirschsprung from other causes of neonatal obstruction.
Contrast enema can suggest Hirschsprung (dilated proximal colon → transition zone → narrow aganglionic distal segment) but is not the gold standard for confirmation.

choices.text: Rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells
choices.feedback: Correct! Rectal suction biopsy: taken 2-3 cm above the dentate line (above the normal zone of physiologic aganglionosis). Findings: (1) absent ganglion cells in submucosal (Meissner) and myenteric (Auerbach) plexuses, (2) hypertrophied acetylcholinesterase-positive nerve fibers. Can be performed at bedside without anesthesia. Anorectal manometry (absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex) supports but does not confirm. Full-thickness biopsy for equivocal cases.

explanation: Best answer: Rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells and nerve trunk hypertrophy.

stem: A horseshoe kidney is found incidentally on imaging. What prevents normal ascent and what is the most common complication?

choices.text: The inferior mesenteric artery traps the fused isthmus, preventing full ascent → UPJ obstruction and stones
choices.feedback: Correct! Horseshoe kidney: lower poles fuse across midline (isthmus) during ascent → trapped by the inferior mesenteric artery at L3-L4 (does not ascend to normal L1-L2 position). Most common renal fusion anomaly (~1:400). Complications: UPJ obstruction (altered ureteral anatomy → hydronephrosis), nephrolithiasis (urinary stasis), UTIs, increased risk of Wilms tumor in children. Most are asymptomatic and discovered incidentally. Associated with Turner syndrome (45,X).

choices.text: The aortic bifurcation prevents descent → ectopic pelvic kidney
choices.feedback: Horseshoe kidneys are trapped during ascent (not descent), and the IMA (not aortic bifurcation) is the blocking structure.

choices.text: Fusion is blocked by the diaphragm → respiratory problems
choices.feedback: The diaphragm does not impede renal ascent. A different abdominal structure traps the fused kidney.
While ureteral anatomy may be altered in horseshoe kidney, bilateral VUR is not the primary complication. UPJ obstruction from the abnormal renal position is more common.

stem: A newborn with coarctation of the aorta becomes critically ill when the ductus arteriosus closes. What is the emergent treatment?

choices.feedback: Indomethacin CLOSES the PDA — this would be catastrophic in ductal-dependent coarctation. PGE1 to OPEN the ductus is needed.
Septostomy is used for D-TGA (to allow mixing). Coarctation requires PDA maintenance, not atrial communication.
Surgical repair is definitive but the patient needs stabilization first. The ductus must be reopened.

choices.text: Prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) infusion to reopen the ductus arteriosus
choices.feedback: Correct! Coarctation of the aorta (juxtaductal): narrowing near the ductus arteriosus → lower body perfusion depends on the PDA. When PDA closes → severe hypoperfusion of lower body → metabolic acidosis, renal failure, NEC. PGE1 (alprostadil): reopens and maintains PDA → restores lower body perfusion. Side effects: apnea (have ventilator ready), fever, hypotension. Definitive: surgical repair (resection + end-to-end anastomosis) or catheter-based intervention once stabilized.

stem: A male fetus at 20 weeks has bilateral hydronephrosis, thick-walled bladder, and oligohydramnios. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Prune belly (Eagle-Barrett) syndrome includes absent abdominal musculature, cryptorchidism, and urinary tract anomalies — but oligohydramnios with keyhole sign on ultrasound is most specific for PUV.
Bilateral UPJ obstruction can cause hydronephrosis but would not explain the thick-walled bladder or oligohydramnios pattern as well as lower tract obstruction.
Bilateral MCDK would show cystic kidneys without a dilated bladder. The thick-walled bladder and urethral dilation point to lower tract obstruction (PUV).

Correct! Male + bilateral hydronephrosis + thick-walled bladder + dilated posterior urethra (keyhole sign) + oligohydramnios = PUV until proven otherwise. Oligohydramnios: decreased fetal urine output → reduced amniotic fluid → risks Potter sequence (pulmonary hypoplasia, limb deformities, facial compression). Prenatal: consider vesicoamniotic shunting if severe oligohydramnios before viability. Postnatal: catheter drainage → cystoscopic valve ablation.

stem: A child with Down syndrome is diagnosed with an atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD). What embryologic structure failed to develop properly?

choices.feedback: Septum secundum defects cause ostium secundum ASD (most common ASD type), not the complete AVSD pattern.
Sinus venosus defects cause sinus venosus ASD (associated with anomalous pulmonary venous drainage), not AVSD.

Correct! Endocardial cushions: mesenchymal tissue in the center of the heart → form the AV septum, mitral and tricuspid valves, and contribute to atrial and ventricular septa. Failed development → AVSD (atrioventricular septal defect, previously called endocardial cushion defect): ostium primum ASD + inlet VSD + common AV valve. Strongly associated with trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) — ~40% of cardiac defects in Down syndrome are AVSD. Treatment: complete surgical repair at 3-6 months.
Neural crest defects cause conotruncal anomalies (truncus arteriosus, TOF), not AVSD.

stem: What is the most common congenital heart defect overall?

choices.feedback: Bicuspid AV is very common (~1-2% of population) but is often classified separately from structural CHD. Among traditional CHD, VSD is most common.
ASD is common but not the most common overall. It is the most common CHD diagnosed in adults.
PDA is common in premature infants but VSD is more prevalent overall.

Correct! VSD is the most common congenital heart defect (~30% of all CHD). Types by location: perimembranous/membranous (most common — 80%), muscular, inlet (endocardial cushion-related), outlet/supracristal. Small VSDs: often close spontaneously (especially muscular). Large VSDs: left-to-right shunt → pulmonary overcirculation → heart failure → if uncorrected, Eisenmenger syndrome (irreversible pulmonary hypertension with shunt reversal → cyanosis). Surgical closure when symptomatic or significant shunt (Qp:Qs > 2:1).

explanation: Correct: Ventricular septal defect (VSD).

stem: A child presents with a midline neck mass that elevates with tongue protrusion. What is the diagnosis and definitive treatment?

choices.feedback: Midline dermoid cysts can occur but do NOT move with tongue protrusion. Movement with swallowing AND tongue protrusion is pathognomonic for thyroglossal.

choices.text: Branchial cleft cyst — excision without hyoid resection
choices.feedback: Branchial cleft cysts are LATERAL (anterior to SCM). This midline mass with tongue protrusion movement is a thyroglossal duct cyst requiring Sistrunk procedure.

choices.text: Reactive lymph node — antibiotics and observation
choices.feedback: Lymph nodes are typically lateral. A midline mass moving with tongue protrusion is essentially diagnostic of thyroglossal duct cyst.

Correct! Thyroglossal duct cyst: remnant of the thyroglossal duct (thyroid descent pathway from foramen cecum to pretracheal position). Key sign: moves with swallowing (attached to hyoid) AND tongue protrusion (attached to base of tongue via tract). Sistrunk procedure: excise cyst + central body of hyoid bone + tract up to foramen cecum (reduces recurrence from 50% with simple excision to <5%). Always confirm normal thyroid tissue elsewhere (ultrasound) — the cyst may contain the patient's ONLY thyroid tissue (lingual thyroid scenario).

explanation: Correct: Thyroglossal duct cyst — Sistrunk procedure (cyst + central hyoid + tract to foramen cecum).

stem: Before performing a Sistrunk procedure for a thyroglossal duct cyst, what preoperative study is essential?

choices.feedback: FNA is rarely needed for classic thyroglossal cysts. It may be considered if malignancy is suspected (rare — 1% papillary thyroid carcinoma in thyroglossal cysts, typically in adults).

choices.text: Thyroid ultrasound to confirm normal thyroid tissue exists in the pretracheal position
choices.feedback: Correct! Critical: the thyroglossal duct cyst may contain ectopic thyroid tissue — and in rare cases (1-2%) this may be the patient's ONLY functioning thyroid tissue (lingual thyroid that failed to descend). Thyroid ultrasound before surgery confirms a normal thyroid gland exists in its usual pretracheal location. If no normal thyroid seen → thyroid scan to locate functioning tissue → avoid excising the only thyroid → patient would become permanently hypothyroid. Also check thyroid function (TSH/T4).

choices.text: CT with contrast of the neck
choices.feedback: CT may be useful for complex cases but is not the essential preoperative study.
Barium swallow evaluates the esophagus. It has no role in thyroglossal duct cyst workup.

stem: What embryologic structure gives rise to the aorticopulmonary septum, and what cell population is critical for its formation?

choices.text: Endocardial cushion tissue from the AV canal
choices.feedback: Endocardial cushions form the AV valves and contribute to atrial/ventricular septation, not the aorticopulmonary septum.

choices.text: Neural crest cells migrating through pharyngeal arches 3, 4, and 6
choices.feedback: Correct! The aorticopulmonary septum divides the truncus arteriosus into the aorta and pulmonary artery. It forms via spiral migration of neural crest cells through pharyngeal arches. Defective neural crest migration → conotruncal defects: Tetralogy of Fallot, transposition of great arteries, truncus arteriosus (persistent), interrupted aortic arch type B. Associated: DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) — neural crest deficiency → conotruncal defects + absent thymus/parathyroids + facial anomalies.
These structures form the interatrial septum, not the conotruncal septum.
Lateral plate mesoderm forms body wall and limb skeleton, not the aorticopulmonary septum.

stem: A neonate with 22q11.2 deletion presents with cyanosis. Echocardiogram shows a single arterial trunk arising from both ventricles with a VSD. What is the cardiac defect?

choices.feedback: TOF has 4 components (VSD, overriding aorta, RV outflow obstruction, RVH) but maintains separate aorta and pulmonary artery.

Correct! Persistent truncus arteriosus: failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to form → single great vessel (truncus) supplying systemic, pulmonary, and coronary circulations + obligate VSD. Classification: Type I (single pulmonary trunk from truncus), Type II (separate pulmonary arteries from posterior truncus), Type III (pulmonary arteries from lateral truncus). Strongly associated with 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge). Treatment: surgical repair in neonatal period.
TGA has TWO separate great arteries arising from the wrong ventricles (aorta from RV, PA from LV), not a single trunk.
TAPVR involves anomalous pulmonary venous drainage, not a single arterial trunk.

stem: What cardiac defect is characterized by the aorta arising from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery from the left ventricle?

choices.feedback: Truncus arteriosus is a single great vessel, not two swapped vessels.
In TOF, the aorta overrides the VSD but still arises primarily from the LV. TGA has complete ventriculoarterial discordance.

Correct! D-TGA: failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral → aorta connects to RV, PA connects to LV → two parallel circuits (incompatible with life without mixing). Presentation: severe cyanosis at birth NOT responsive to supplemental O2. Survival depends on mixing: PFO, ASD, VSD, or PDA. Emergency: prostaglandin E1 (maintains PDA), balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind procedure). Definitive: arterial switch operation (Jatene procedure) within first 2 weeks.
Ebstein anomaly involves apical displacement of the tricuspid valve, not great vessel transposition.

stem: A neonate presents with bilious vomiting on day 2 of life. Upper GI series shows the duodenojejunal junction (ligament of Treitz) displaced to the right of midline. What is the diagnosis?

choices.text: Intestinal malrotation with possible midgut volvulus
choices.feedback: Correct! Malrotation: failure of the midgut to complete its 270° counterclockwise rotation during weeks 6-10 → abnormal cecal position → Ladd bands (peritoneal bands crossing duodenum) + narrow mesenteric base → risk of midgut volvulus. Bilious vomiting in a neonate = malrotation/volvulus until proven otherwise (surgical emergency). Diagnosis: upper GI series (gold standard) — abnormal position of ligament of Treitz. If volvulus suspected: emergent surgical exploration (Ladd procedure) without delay for imaging.
Pyloric stenosis causes NON-bilious, projectile vomiting at 3-6 weeks of age. Bilious vomiting in a neonate = malrotation until proven otherwise.
NEC typically occurs in premature infants, with pneumatosis intestinalis on X-ray, not isolated bilious vomiting with malpositioned ligament of Treitz.
Duodenal atresia shows the classic 'double bubble' sign on X-ray and presents at birth. The UGI finding of displaced ligament of Treitz indicates malrotation.

stem: What embryologic event underlies intestinal malrotation?

choices.text: Failure of the midgut to return to the abdominal cavity from the physiologic umbilical herniation
choices.feedback: Failure to return to the abdomen causes omphalocele/gastroschisis, not malrotation. The midgut returns but rotates incompletely.

choices.text: Defective neural crest cell migration to the intestinal wall
choices.feedback: Neural crest migration failure causes Hirschsprung disease (aganglionic bowel), not malrotation.

choices.text: Premature closure of the omphalomesenteric duct
choices.feedback: Persistence of the omphalomesenteric (vitelline) duct causes Meckel diverticulum, not malrotation.

choices.text: Incomplete 270° counterclockwise rotation of the midgut around the superior mesenteric artery
choices.feedback: Correct! Normal midgut development: weeks 6-10, midgut herniates into umbilical cord, rotates 270° counterclockwise around the SMA axis, then returns to abdomen. Incomplete rotation → cecum fails to reach RLQ → Ladd bands form (compress duodenum), mesenteric base is narrow (instead of broad) → susceptible to volvulus. Types: nonrotation (most common — midgut entirely on the right), incomplete rotation, reverse rotation (rare). Risk of volvulus is highest in infancy.

stem: What surgical procedure corrects intestinal malrotation?

choices.feedback: Pull-through (Soave or Swenson) is for Hirschsprung disease, not malrotation.
Pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt procedure) is for pyloric stenosis, not malrotation.

Correct! Ladd procedure: (1) reduce volvulus (counterclockwise detorsion), (2) divide Ladd bands (crossing the duodenum), (3) broaden the mesenteric base, (4) appendectomy (appendix will be in abnormal position — prevents future diagnostic confusion), (5) place small bowel on right, colon on left (nonrotation position). Emergent if volvulus present — ischemic bowel can progress to necrosis rapidly. If bowel viability questionable: second-look laparotomy in 24-48 hours.
Kasai procedure is for biliary atresia, not intestinal malrotation.

stem: What is the most feared complication of untreated intestinal malrotation?

choices.feedback: Constipation is not a feature of malrotation. The feared complication is acute vascular compromise from volvulus.
Ladd bands compress the duodenum causing obstruction, but perforation is not the primary feared outcome. Vascular compromise from volvulus is.
Intussusception can occur but is not the primary feared complication unique to malrotation.

choices.text: Midgut volvulus leading to intestinal necrosis and short bowel syndrome
choices.feedback: Correct! The narrow mesenteric base in malrotation allows the entire midgut to twist around the SMA (volvulus) → compromises blood supply to the midgut → if not corrected within hours → massive intestinal necrosis → short bowel syndrome (lifelong TPN dependence) or death. This is why bilious vomiting in a neonate is a surgical emergency. Volvulus can also occur intermittently in older children/adults with chronic abdominal pain episodes.

explanation: Best answer: Midgut volvulus leading to intestinal necrosis and short bowel syndrome.

stem: A newborn fails to pass meconium in the first 48 hours and develops abdominal distension. Barium enema shows a transition zone in the rectosigmoid. What confirms the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Upper GI evaluates the proximal GI tract. Hirschsprung disease involves the distal colon.
Anorectal manometry (absent recto-anal inhibitory reflex) is supportive but not confirmatory. Biopsy is the gold standard.
CT is not diagnostic for Hirschsprung. Rectal biopsy demonstrating absent ganglion cells is required.

choices.text: Rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells and hypertrophied nerve trunks
choices.feedback: Correct! Rectal suction biopsy is the gold standard for Hirschsprung disease diagnosis: absence of ganglion cells in the submucosal (Meissner) and myenteric (Auerbach) plexuses + nerve trunk hypertrophy + positive acetylcholinesterase staining. Pathophysiology: failure of neural crest cell migration to the distal intestine → aganglionic segment cannot relax → functional obstruction. Most common: rectosigmoid (80%). Associated: Down syndrome (5-10% of Hirschsprung), MEN2A/RET mutations.

explanation: Best answer: Rectal suction biopsy showing absent ganglion cells and hypertrophied nerve trunks.

stem: What is the definitive treatment for Hirschsprung disease?

choices.feedback: Laxatives cannot overcome a mechanical obstruction from aganglionic bowel. Surgical resection is required.

choices.text: Surgical pull-through procedure removing the aganglionic segment
choices.feedback: Correct! Pull-through procedure: resect the aganglionic bowel and anastomose normally innervated (ganglionic) bowel to the anus. Techniques: Soave (endorectal), Swenson (full-thickness), Duhamel (retrorectal). Most now done as single-stage in neonates. If the child is too ill: initial decompressing colostomy → delayed pull-through. Complications: enterocolitis (Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis — HAEC — most common serious complication post-operatively), stricture, fecal incontinence. Long-term outcomes generally excellent.

choices.text: Botulinum toxin injection to the internal anal sphincter
choices.feedback: Botox may provide temporary relief but is not definitive therapy. Surgical removal of the aganglionic segment is required.
Appendectomy has no role in Hirschsprung disease treatment. The aganglionic bowel segment must be resected.

stem: What embryologic event causes horseshoe kidney, and what structure prevents normal ascent?

choices.text: Agenesis of one kidney with compensatory hypertrophy of the other
choices.feedback: That describes unilateral renal agenesis, not horseshoe kidney.

choices.text: Failure of the pronephros to degenerate
choices.feedback: The pronephros is a transient structure that degenerates normally. Horseshoe kidney involves the metanephros (definitive kidney).

choices.text: Fusion of the lower poles during ascent, trapped by the inferior mesenteric artery
choices.feedback: Correct! Horseshoe kidney: the metanephric blastemas fuse at their lower poles before or during ascent from the pelvis → the fused isthmus gets caught beneath the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) at L3 → kidneys remain low. Most common renal fusion anomaly (~1:400). Usually asymptomatic. Associated: Turner syndrome (45,X), UPJ obstruction (due to high ureteral insertion), increased risk of Wilms tumor and renal calculi. Most are found incidentally on imaging.

choices.text: Duplication of the ureteric bud → double collecting system
choices.feedback: Ureteric bud duplication causes duplex kidneys, not horseshoe kidney.

stem: Horseshoe kidney is strongly associated with which chromosomal condition?

choices.feedback: Correct! Turner syndrome has a strong association with horseshoe kidney (~10-20% of Turner patients). Other renal anomalies in Turner: duplicated collecting systems, renal agenesis. Turner features: 45,X karyotype, short stature, webbed neck, coarctation of aorta, streak gonads, lymphedema, shield chest. Horseshoe kidney can also occur sporadically and is usually asymptomatic. Monitor for UPJ obstruction and urinary tract infections.
Trisomy 18 is associated with many anomalies (clenched fists, rocker-bottom feet, cardiac defects) but Turner syndrome has the strongest horseshoe kidney association.
Klinefelter is associated with tall stature, gynecomastia, and infertility, not horseshoe kidney.
Down syndrome is associated with duodenal atresia, AV canal defects, and Hirschsprung, not classically with horseshoe kidney.

stem: What is the most common complication of horseshoe kidney?

choices.feedback: Horseshoe kidney isthmus can be vulnerable to blunt trauma but spontaneous rupture is extremely rare.

Correct! The abnormal anatomy of horseshoe kidney — high ureteral insertion and altered drainage angle — predisposes to UPJ obstruction and stone formation. Urinary stasis → infection (recurrent UTIs) and calculi. Other associations: increased risk of Wilms tumor (children), transitional cell carcinoma. Most horseshoe kidneys are asymptomatic and found incidentally, but symptomatic patients present with flank pain, UTIs, or hematuria.
Wilms tumor risk is slightly increased but renal cell carcinoma is not the most common complication.
Horseshoe kidney function is generally normal. CKD is not a common complication unless complicated by obstruction or recurrent infection.

stem: A neonate with coarctation of the aorta develops severe cyanosis and shock when the ductus arteriosus closes. What medication maintains ductal patency?

choices.feedback: Indomethacin CLOSES the PDA (prostaglandin inhibitor). This patient needs the PDA kept OPEN.
Sildenafil is a pulmonary vasodilator (PDE5 inhibitor) used for pulmonary hypertension, not to maintain PDA patency.
Digoxin improves cardiac contractility but does not affect ductal patency.

Correct! PGE1 maintains PDA patency by preventing smooth muscle constriction of the ductus arteriosus. Critical for ductal-dependent lesions where systemic or pulmonary blood flow depends on the PDA. Ductal-dependent systemic flow: critical coarctation, interrupted aortic arch, hypoplastic left heart. Ductal-dependent pulmonary flow: critical pulmonary stenosis, pulmonary atresia, tricuspid atresia. Side effects: apnea (monitor, may need intubation), hypotension, fever. Continue until surgical or catheter-based intervention.

stem: What physical exam finding is classic for coarctation of the aorta?

choices.feedback: Continuous machinery murmur is the hallmark of PDA, not coarctation. Coarctation produces a systolic murmur best heard in the left interscapular area.

choices.text: Wide pulse pressure in all extremities
choices.feedback: Wide pulse pressure suggests aortic regurgitation. Coarctation has differential blood pressure between upper and lower extremities.

choices.text: Upper extremity hypertension with diminished or absent femoral pulses and a systolic blood pressure gradient >20 mmHg between arms and legs
choices.feedback: Correct! Coarctation creates a mechanical obstruction (usually at the aortic isthmus near the ductus/ligamentum arteriosum) → high pressure proximal to the coarctation (upper extremities) and low pressure distal (lower extremities). Classic: blood pressure difference >20 mmHg upper vs. lower extremity, brachial-femoral pulse delay, weak/absent femoral pulses. CXR: '3 sign' (indentation at coarctation site), rib notching (collateral intercostal arteries eroding ribs — seen in older children/adults). Associated: bicuspid aortic valve (50-80%), Turner syndrome.
Fixed split S2 is characteristic of ASD, not coarctation.

explanation: Best answer: Upper extremity hypertension with diminished or absent femoral pulses and a systolic blood pressure gradient >20 mmHg between arms and legs.

stem: What embryologic remnant does the ductus arteriosus become after closure?

choices.feedback: Ligamentum teres is the remnant of the umbilical VEIN, not the ductus arteriosus.

Correct! The ductus arteriosus (connects pulmonary artery to aorta in fetal circulation, allowing blood to bypass the non-functioning lungs) closes within 24-48 hours after birth in response to increased O2 and decreased prostaglandins → fibroses into the ligamentum arteriosum. Failure to close = PDA. Other fetal shunt remnants: umbilical vein → ligamentum teres hepatis, ductus venosus → ligamentum venosum, umbilical arteries → medial umbilical ligaments, foramen ovale → fossa ovalis.
Fossa ovalis is the remnant of the foramen ovale (interatrial shunt), not the ductus arteriosus.
Ligamentum venosum is the remnant of the ductus venosus, not the ductus arteriosus.

stem: A male neonate presents with bilateral hydronephrosis, a palpable distended bladder, and poor urinary stream. Prenatal ultrasound showed bilateral hydroureter and oligohydramnios. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: VUR causes hydronephrosis but typically does not present with palpable bladder distension or oligohydramnios this severe.
Prune belly (Eagle-Barrett) syndrome shows absent abdominal wall musculature, cryptorchidism, and urinary tract anomalies. It can coexist with PUV but is a separate diagnosis.
UPJ obstruction causes unilateral hydronephrosis (rarely bilateral) and does not cause bladder distension or urethral obstruction.

Correct! PUV: most common cause of severe obstructive uropathy in male neonates. Abnormal tissue folds in the posterior urethra (near verumontanum) → bladder outlet obstruction → bilateral hydroureter/hydronephrosis → renal dysplasia (if severe/prolonged) → oligohydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia (Potter sequence). Occurs ONLY in males. Diagnosis: VCUG (voiding cystourethrography) — dilated posterior urethra with abrupt caliber change. Treatment: emergent catheter drainage → surgical valve ablation (endoscopic).

stem: What prenatal finding raises suspicion for posterior urethral valves?

choices.text: Bilateral hydronephrosis, distended bladder ('keyhole sign'), and oligohydramnios in a male fetus
choices.feedback: Correct! Prenatal ultrasound clues for PUV: bilateral hydronephrosis, thick-walled distended bladder with keyhole sign (dilated posterior urethra), oligohydramnios. All occur in MALE fetuses only. Oligohydramnios is concerning because fetal urine is the major contributor to amniotic fluid in the 2nd/3rd trimester → deficiency leads to pulmonary hypoplasia and limb compression (Potter sequence). Severity of renal dysplasia at birth determines long-term prognosis (30% develop ESRD).
Echogenic bowel suggests cystic fibrosis, Down syndrome, or CMV infection, not urinary tract obstruction.
Single umbilical artery is associated with various anomalies but is not specific to PUV.
PUV causes urinary obstruction → decreased fetal urine production → OLIGOHYDRAMNIOS, not polyhydramnios.

explanation: Best answer: Bilateral hydronephrosis, distended bladder ('keyhole sign'), and oligohydramnios in a male fetus.

stem: What long-term renal complication most commonly results from posterior urethral valves?

choices.feedback: While tubular dysfunction can occur, the major long-term outcome is progressive CKD leading to ESRD.

Correct! PUV is the most common cause of ESRD from obstructive uropathy in children. ~30% develop ESRD by adolescence despite early intervention. Mechanism: prolonged back-pressure → renal dysplasia (irreversible structural damage occurring in utero), ongoing bladder dysfunction (thick-walled, poorly compliant 'valve bladder') → persistent reflux and hydronephrosis. Long-term management: monitor renal function, manage bladder dysfunction (CIC if needed), eventual renal transplant for ESRD. Nadir creatinine at 1 year of age is the best predictor of long-term renal outcome.
PUV does not predispose to malignancy.
PUV causes obstructive/dysplastic nephropathy, not glomerular disease leading to nephrotic syndrome.

explanation: Best answer: Chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease.

stem: What type of ASD is the most common, and what embryologic structure is deficient?

choices.text: Secundum ASD — deficient septum primum in the region of the fossa ovalis
choices.feedback: Correct! Secundum ASD (~70% of all ASDs): excessive resorption of the septum primum OR inadequate development of the septum secundum → defect in the fossa ovalis region. Most common type overall. Hemodynamics: left-to-right shunt → RV volume overload → fixed split S2 (hallmark — no respiratory variation). Usually asymptomatic in childhood. Complications if uncorrected: right heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, paradoxical embolism, atrial arrhythmias. Closure: percutaneous device (Amplatzer) or surgical.

choices.text: Coronary sinus ASD — unroofed coronary sinus
choices.feedback: Coronary sinus defects are the rarest type of ASD (<1%).

choices.text: Sinus venosus ASD — near the SVC or IVC entry
choices.feedback: Sinus venosus ASDs (~10%) occur near the SVC (most common) or IVC junction and are associated with anomalous pulmonary venous return. Not the most common type.

choices.text: Primum ASD — deficient septum primum near AV valves
choices.feedback: Primum ASDs (~20%) are associated with endocardial cushion defects and AV valve abnormalities (common in Down syndrome). The MOST common type involves a different structure.

stem: A child is found to have a harsh holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border with a systolic thrill. Echocardiogram shows a small perimembranous VSD. What is the natural history?

choices.text: VSD will inevitably enlarge and cause Eisenmenger syndrome
choices.feedback: Small VSDs rarely progress to Eisenmenger syndrome. Only large unrepaired VSDs with longstanding left-to-right shunt lead to irreversible pulmonary hypertension.

choices.text: All VSDs require antibiotic prophylaxis for life
choices.feedback: Current AHA guidelines do NOT recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis for isolated VSDs unless there is a prior history of endocarditis or specific high-risk features.

choices.text: Small VSDs often close spontaneously by age 2
choices.feedback: Correct! Small (restrictive) VSDs: high-velocity, loud murmur (high pressure gradient across small defect) but minimal hemodynamic impact. ~60-80% of small muscular VSDs and ~35% of perimembranous VSDs close spontaneously. Large (nonrestrictive) VSDs: cause significant left-to-right shunt → CHF symptoms (FTT, tachypnea, diaphoresis with feeding) → require surgical or percutaneous closure. Indications for closure: Qp:Qs >1.5:1, heart failure, failure to thrive, pulmonary hypertension. Unrepaired large VSD → Eisenmenger syndrome (irreversible).
Small VSDs with minimal hemodynamic impact are managed conservatively. Many close spontaneously.

explanation: Key detail: 5; loud murmur paradoxically indicates a smaller, restrictive defect.

stem: What is the embryologic origin of the membranous interventricular septum?

choices.feedback: Neural crest cells contribute to the conotruncal septum (aorticopulmonary), not the interventricular membranous septum primarily.

choices.text: Septum primum tissue extending into the ventricle
choices.feedback: Septum primum forms the interATRIAL septum, not the interventricular septum.
The bulbus cordis contributes to the conus arteriosus/outflow tract, not the interventricular septum.

choices.text: Fusion of the endocardial cushions with the muscular septum
choices.feedback: Correct! The interventricular septum has two components: (1) Muscular septum — grows upward from the ventricular floor (forms the bulk), (2) Membranous septum — the final small portion closed by fusion of the endocardial cushions (AV canal), muscular septum, and conotruncal (aorticopulmonary) ridges. Perimembranous VSDs (~80% of all VSDs) occur when this fusion is incomplete. Muscular VSDs (~20%) are holes in the muscular portion. Endocardial cushion defects → inlet/primum-type VSDs (associated with Down syndrome).

explanation: Best answer: Fusion of the endocardial cushions with the muscular septum and conotruncal ridges.

stem: A child presents with a midline neck mass that moves upward with swallowing and tongue protrusion. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Ectopic thyroid (lingual thyroid) presents as a mass at the BASE OF THE TONGUE, not in the neck. Must confirm normal thyroid tissue exists before excising.
Branchial cleft cysts are LATERAL, along the anterior border of the SCM. Midline + moves with swallowing = thyroglossal.

Correct! Thyroglossal duct cyst: most common congenital midline neck mass. The thyroid descends from the foramen cecum (base of tongue) through the hyoid bone → thyroglossal duct normally involutes. Persistent duct → cyst formation (65% infrahyoid, 20% suprahyoid, 15% at hyoid). Pathognomonic: midline, moves with swallowing AND tongue protrusion (attached to hyoid/foramen cecum). Treatment: Sistrunk procedure (excise cyst + central hyoid bone + tract to foramen cecum) — reduces recurrence from 50% to <5%.
Dermoid cysts are midline but do NOT move with tongue protrusion. They are not connected to the hyoid bone.

stem: What pre-operative study is essential before excising a thyroglossal duct cyst?

choices.text: MRI of the brain to evaluate pituitary function
choices.feedback: Pituitary imaging has no role in thyroglossal duct cyst evaluation.
FNA may be performed if malignancy is suspected (solid component on imaging) but is not the ESSENTIAL pre-operative study. Confirming normal thyroid location takes priority.

choices.text: Thyroid ultrasound to confirm a normal thyroid gland is present in the usual position
choices.feedback: Correct! Essential pre-op: confirm that a normally positioned thyroid gland exists. In ~1% of cases, the thyroglossal duct cyst may contain the patient's ONLY functioning thyroid tissue. Excising it would cause permanent hypothyroidism. Thyroid ultrasound (or nuclear medicine scan) confirms eutopic thyroid tissue before surgery. The Sistrunk procedure removes the cyst, its tract, and the central portion of the hyoid bone to prevent recurrence. Also: 1% of thyroglossal duct cysts contain papillary thyroid carcinoma.
Vascular imaging is not routinely needed for thyroglossal duct cyst evaluation.

stem: A 2nd branchial cleft cyst presents as a lateral neck mass along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid. What pharyngeal pouch anomaly would present with immunodeficiency and hypocalcemia?

choices.feedback: The 6th arch forms laryngeal cartilages and the recurrent laryngeal nerve. It does not relate to immune function or calcium.
The 1st pharyngeal pouch forms the middle ear and Eustachian tube. Its anomalies don't cause immunodeficiency.

Correct! The 3rd pharyngeal pouch → inferior parathyroids + thymus. The 4th pouch → superior parathyroids + parafollicular C cells. DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion): deficient 3rd/4th pouch development → absent/hypoplastic thymus (T-cell immunodeficiency) + absent/hypoplastic parathyroids (hypocalcemia) + conotruncal cardiac defects + facial anomalies. Mnemonic: CATCH-22 (Cardiac defects, Abnormal facies, Thymic aplasia, Cleft palate, Hypocalcemia — chromosome 22).
The 2nd pharyngeal pouch forms the palatine tonsils. Its anomalies cause tonsillar tissue abnormalities, not immunodeficiency.

explanation: Correct: 3rd and 4th pharyngeal pouch anomaly (DiGeorge syndrome).

stem: PDA closure is achieved pharmacologically with:

choices.feedback: PGE1 KEEPS the PDA open — used in duct-dependent lesions.

Correct! NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis → PDA closure. Indomethacin or ibuprofen IV for premature infants with hemodynamically significant PDA. PGE1 (alprostadil) does the OPPOSITE — maintains PDA patency in duct-dependent congenital heart defects (transposition, critical coarctation, hypoplastic left heart). Surgical ligation if medical therapy fails.
Furosemide is a diuretic used for volume management, not PDA closure.
Digoxin treats heart failure symptoms but doesn't close the PDA.

explanation:
PDA closure: NSAIDs (indomethacin/ibuprofen) inhibit prostaglandins. PGE1 (alprostadil) does opposite — maintains patency for duct-dependent lesions. Surgical ligation if medical therapy fails.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK430758] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430758/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: The most common congenital heart defect is:

choices.feedback: TOF is the most common cyanotic CHD, but not the most common overall.

Correct! VSD accounts for ~25-30% of all CHD. Most common location: membranous (perimembranous) septum. Small VSDs often close spontaneously. Large VSDs → left-to-right shunt → volume overload → CHF, failure to thrive, recurrent infections. If uncorrected → Eisenmenger syndrome (irreversible pulmonary hypertension → shunt reversal → cyanosis). Holosystolic murmur at LLSB.
ASD is the most common CHD diagnosed in adults but not the most common overall.
PDA is common in premature infants but not the most common CHD overall.

explanation:
VSD: most common CHD (~25-30%). Most common location: membranous septum. Small VSDs may close spontaneously. Large → CHF → Eisenmenger if uncorrected. Holosystolic murmur at LLSB.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK470330] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470330/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: The VACTERL association in anorectal malformations includes all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: V = Vertebral anomalies.
C = Cardiac defects.

Correct! VACTERL: Vertebral, Anorectal, Cardiac, Tracheo-Esophageal fistula, Renal, Limb anomalies. No H for hepatic. Must have ≥3 components for the association. Anorectal malformations range from low (perineal fistula, good prognosis) to high (rectovesical fistula, complex repair). All newborns with imperforate anus need full VACTERL workup: spinal US, echocardiogram, renal US, limb X-rays.
L = Limb anomalies.

explanation:
VACTERL: Vertebral, Anorectal, Cardiac, Tracheo-Esophageal, Renal, Limb. No hepatic component. Need ≥3 for association. Full workup for any imperforate anus.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK535370] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK535370/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Wingspread classification of anorectal malformations divides them into:

choices.feedback: Laterality is not the classification system.

choices.text: High, intermediate, and low based on relationship to the levator muscle
choices.feedback: Correct! Wingspread classification: Low (below levator → perineal fistula, anterior anus — good continence prognosis, perineal anoplasty), Intermediate (at levator level), High (above levator → rectovesical, rectourethral, or rectovaginal fistula — require posterior sagittal anorectoplasty/PSARP, higher incontinence risk). Krickenbeck classification is more commonly used now for standardized reporting.
All ARM are congenital; this isn't the classification basis.
All ARM are symptomatic at birth.

explanation:
ARM classification: low (below levator, good prognosis), intermediate, high (above levator, PSARP needed). Krickenbeck now preferred. Low = perineal anoplasty.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK535370] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK535370/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: An infant with Down syndrome is found to have an AVSD (atrioventricular septal defect). The embryological basis for this defect is:

choices.text: Failure of endocardial cushion fusion, which forms the AV septum and AV valves
choices.feedback: Correct! Endocardial cushions fuse to form the lower atrial septum (septum primum), the membranous interventricular septum, and the mitral/tricuspid valves. Failure → AVSD (common in trisomy 21). Complete AVSD: single AV valve with large atrial + ventricular communication.
Neural crest defects cause conotruncal anomalies (Tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus), not AVSD.

choices.text: Failure of muscular ventricular septum growth
choices.feedback: Muscular VSD is a separate entity from endocardial cushion defects.

choices.text: Abnormal looping of the heart tube
choices.feedback: Looping defects cause dextrocardia or heterotaxy, not AVSD.

explanation:
Endocardial cushions are mesenchymal swellings in the AV canal that contribute to: (1) lower portion of atrial septum (septum primum fuses with cushions), (2) membranous interventricular septum, (3) septal leaflets of mitral and tricuspid valves. Failure of cushion development → AVSD spectrum: partial (ostium primum ASD + cleft mitral valve) or complete (large central defect with single AV valve). Down syndrome association: ~40-50% of infants with complete AVSD have trisomy 21, and AVSD is the most common CHD in Down syndrome. Other trisomy 21-associated cardiac defects: VSD, ASD, PDA, tetralogy of Fallot.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK537313] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537313/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A neonate presents with bilious vomiting and abdominal distension. X-ray shows dilated loops of bowel with multiple air-fluid levels and no distal gas. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Jejunal/ileal atresia presents with bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, and multiple air-fluid levels with absent distal gas. Unlike duodenal atresia (which shows 'double bubble'), jejunal atresia shows multiple dilated loops. It is caused by in utero mesenteric vascular accident (not a failure of recanalization like duodenal atresia). Treatment: surgical resection and primary anastomosis. [StatPearls NBK536937]

feedback.correct: Correct! Multiple air-fluid levels + no distal gas = jejunal atresia from in utero vascular accident.
feedback.incorrect: Multiple dilated loops + absent distal gas = jejunal atresia. Double bubble only = duodenal atresia.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: What is the pathogenesis of jejunal and ileal atresia, and how does it differ from duodenal atresia?

choices.text: Both result from failure of intestinal recanalization
Jejunal/ileal = vascular accident; duodenal = failure of recanalization
Jejunal/ileal = recanalization failure; duodenal = vascular accident

explanation: Jejunal and ileal atresia result from in utero mesenteric vascular accidents (ischemic necrosis and resorption of bowel segment). Duodenal atresia results from failure of recanalization of the solid duodenal cord during weeks 8-10. This distinction explains why duodenal atresia is associated with Down syndrome (21%) and other anomalies, while jejunal/ileal atresia typically occurs in isolation. [StatPearls NBK536937]

feedback.correct: Correct! Jejunal/ileal = vascular accident (isolated). Duodenal = recanalization failure (associated with T21).
feedback.incorrect: Different mechanisms: vascular accident for jejunal/ileal, failed recanalization for duodenal.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A neonate with jejunal atresia has a family history of cystic fibrosis. Which associated finding should be evaluated?
explanation: Meconium ileus (distal ileal obstruction from inspissated meconium) occurs in 15-20% of CF neonates and can coexist with or mimic intestinal atresia. Thick meconium can cause volvulus or perforation leading to atresia. All neonates with intestinal atresia, especially ileal, should be evaluated for cystic fibrosis with sweat chloride testing. [StatPearls NBK536937]

feedback.correct: Correct! CF → meconium ileus → can cause/coexist with atresia. Test for CF in all neonatal intestinal atresia.
feedback.incorrect: Meconium ileus + CF commonly coexist with ileal atresia. Sweat chloride testing indicated.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A male neonate has bilateral hydronephrosis, distended bladder, and a keyhole sign on prenatal ultrasound. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Posterior urethral valves (PUV) are the most common cause of lower urinary tract obstruction in male neonates. The 'keyhole sign' on US shows dilated posterior urethra + distended bladder. PUV are mucosal folds in the prostatic urethra that act as one-way valves. They cause bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, bladder distension, and can lead to renal dysplasia if severe. Diagnosed by voiding cystourethrography (VCUG). Treatment: endoscopic valve ablation. [StatPearls NBK545154]

feedback.correct: Correct! Keyhole sign + bilateral hydro + male = PUV. Most common cause of congenital lower urinary obstruction in boys.
feedback.incorrect: Keyhole sign on US (dilated urethra + bladder) in a male neonate = PUV until proven otherwise.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: What is the gold standard diagnostic study for posterior urethral valves?
explanation: VCUG is the gold standard — it demonstrates the dilated posterior urethra, trabeculated bladder, and often vesicoureteral reflux. Renal US shows hydronephrosis but cannot visualize the valves themselves. Cystoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic (allows endoscopic ablation). Prenatal US may show oligohydramnios, bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, and the keyhole sign. [StatPearls NBK545154]

feedback.correct: Correct! VCUG shows the dilated posterior urethra and valve. Also reveals VUR if present.
feedback.incorrect: VCUG is diagnostic — shows dilated posterior urethra. US shows hydro but can't see the valves. Cystoscopy is for treatment.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Severe prenatal posterior urethral valve obstruction with oligohydramnios can lead to which constellation of findings?
explanation: Severe bilateral obstruction → oligohydramnios → Potter sequence: pulmonary hypoplasia (main cause of mortality), flattened facies, limb contractures. The fetal kidneys produce most amniotic fluid in the 2nd/3rd trimester, so obstruction reduces fluid volume. Bilateral renal dysplasia from backpressure can cause chronic kidney disease. Up to 30% of PUV patients eventually need renal transplantation. [StatPearls NBK545154]

feedback.correct: Correct! Severe PUV → oligohydramnios → Potter sequence. Pulmonary hypoplasia is the major cause of neonatal death.
feedback.incorrect: Obstruction → no urine → no amniotic fluid → Potter sequence (lung hypoplasia, limb deformity, flat facies).

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use
